abstract.t: The modern web lacks a reliable mechanism for verifying the identity of HTTP user agents. Existing methods such as User-Agent strings and IP-based attribution are insufficient due to spoofing, shared infrastructure, and automated agents.
This document specifies SAIP (Signed Agent Identity Protocol), a lightweight, opt-in mechanism for verifiable client identity at the application layer.

section.title: Introduction
section.t: SAIP introduces a lightweight, opt-in mechanism for verifiable client identity at the application layer, enabling servers to distinguish between legitimate automated traffic and malicious actors.

section.title: Problem Statement
section.t: User-Agent strings are trivial to manipulate.
IP-based filtering is unreliable for NAT users and shared infrastructure.
Automated systems are frequently blocked by generic security rules.
Lack of client-level identity enables abuse and spam.

section.title: Protocol Overview
section.t: SAIP defines a cryptographic identity signal transmitted via a single HTTP-style header. It supplements existing authentication systems and enables more accurate trust decisions.

section.title: Header Format
section.sourcecode: SAIP: id="<ID>"; alg="<ALG>"; ts="<TS>"; nonce="<NONCE>"; [pk="<PK>"]; sig="<SIG>"

section.title: Canonicalization and Signature
section.t: The signature binds identity to a specific request to prevent replay across endpoints.

section.title: Processing Model
section.t: The client constructs and signs the canonical string and sends it in the SAIP header. The server reconstructs the string, verifies the signature, and applies policy decisions.

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests registration of the "SAIP" HTTP header field.
